channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Is Cuba Trump’s Next Target?
item.description: The regime in Havana is increasingly in the Trump administration’s crosshairs.

item.title: How Iran Gained the Upper Hand
item.description: And what that means for Washington’s options to extract itself from this quagmire.

item.title: Is the Red Sea the New Hormuz Crisis?
item.description: Houthi attacks on Saudi shipping risk adding a new front to the Iran war.

item.title: China Is AI-Maxxing
item.description: Beijing may be closing the gap with Washington in the AI race.

item.title: Odysseus at the End of Civilization
item.description: Nolan’s film confronts the betrayal of the West’s own values.

item.title: There’s Only One Way Out of Trump’s Reckless War
item.description: Pay off Iran to open Hormuz and freeze its nuclear program.

item.title: Xi’s Demands for Loyalty Are Breeding Corruption
item.description: Ma Xingrui’s fall shows how deep personnel problems run.

item.title: Trump Is Triggering a Middle East Nuclear Arms Race
item.description: His deal to provide nuclear technology to Saudi Arabia encourages weaponization.

item.title: The Strong Suffer What They Must
item.description: Foolish military interventions driven by outdated calculations of economic power have thoroughly discredited the old big powers. Thucydides might well have appreciated the irony of a world in which smaller, weaker countries like Ukraine and Iran can expose Russia and the United States, respectively, as paper tigers.

item.title: Why China Is Investing in Planetary Defense
item.description: For China, planetary defense is about building its international image and strengthening national security. However, the same factors also constrain China's global contribution.

item.title: Nepali PM Balendra Shah’s Image as a Change Agent is at Risk
item.description: Shah has shown less concern for the health and wellbeing of the poor, and is preoccupied with appearing as a ruthless executor of public plans.

item.title: Beijing Is Celebrating a Race It Never Ran
item.description: A new Pew survey shows the world likes China more than the United States. Look closer, and the win belongs to nobody.

item.title: Britain Is Losing the Falklands
item.description: Will Argentina capitalize on growing momentum behind its claim to the islands?

item.title: Lebanon has a chance to break free of Hizbullah
item.description: With better government, it could become a business hub once again

item.title: The Philippines war on drugs outlasts the Duterte presidency
item.description: It continues, rebranded, under Ferdinand “Bongbong” Marcos

item.title: A gold-bar scandal pits Indonesia’s army against its police
item.description: Cracks appear in the court of President Prabowo

item.title: Japan’s popular prime minister is burning political capital fast
item.description: Markets and the Diet are mounting resistance

item.title: Cartoon:
item.description: A lighter take on the news

item.title: Business
Politics
Dismissing Europe as a museum is glib. Think of it as a theme park
item.description: All the continent’s a stage

item.title: Spain’s team embodies footballing values FIFA has betrayed
item.description: La Roja won the World Cup through teamwork and grit

item.title: A surrogacy scandal throws Germany’s conservatives into turmoil
item.description: Just when Friedrich Merz’s government was getting its act together

item.title: For Russians, the war feels closer than ever before
item.description: Strikes on e-commerce hubs are a sign that Ukraine’s strategy against Russia is shifting

item.title: Investors cool on Europe’s old-style defence firms
item.description: Blame supply-chain snags, procurement obstacles and the rise of drone warfare

item.title: Airlines are spending ever more to maintain their fleets
item.description: Engine-makers are earning big bucks as a result

item.title: How African motorists are adapting to the fuel crunch
item.description: Electric vehicles should save both drivers and governments money

item.title: Why young Africans still listen to the radio
item.description: It’s cheap, easy and needs no mobile data

item.title: The return of the African megaproject
item.description: Could a new era of big infrastructure developments accelerate growth?

item.title: Gulf countries are experts at air-conditioning
item.description: They want to sell their technology to the world

item.title: China is gaining troubling military know-how from Russia
item.description: It is learning how to improve its drones, nuclear subs and air defence

item.title: The Chinese economic experiment that failed
item.description: Shantou was once at the forefront of reforms. Now it is a backwater

item.title: The geopolitics of air-conditioning
item.description: Heatwaves are making access to AC a political problem around the world

item.title: How the American right came to hate empathy
item.description: …while practising its oldest form

item.title: The faltering effort to revive copper mining in Michigan
item.description: Riches sit under a rare vestige of the wild Midwest

item.title: Donald Trump is shifting America’s military footprint
item.description: But there are still few signs of a serious pivot to Asia

item.title: Cartoon: America’s trouble getting out of the Iran conflict
item.description: A lighter take on the news

item.title: Airplane-engine maintenance is now a money spinner
item.description: Manufacturers are earning big bucks as a result

item.title: Yes, the Hong Kong of Old Is Over
item.description: The pro-China camp continues to tout Hong Kong’s resilience, recently pointing to the fact that the city has reclaimed its position as the global IPO leader. But conveniently left out of this narrative is that resilience has come to depend on the direct intervention of the Chinese authorities.

item.title: Why Iran Won’t Change
item.description: Embedded in the Islamic Republic’s foundations is a doctrine that allows for total repression of the population and mobilization of all state resources to preserve the system. This helps to explain why the US and Israel were wrong to expect the regime to change its behavior, let alone be brought down by the Iranian people.

item.title: Narendra Modi’s party discovers the limits of propaganda
item.description: India’s biggest protest in years has produced shocking videos and angry young people

item.title: Should you be afraid of Elon Musk?
item.description: Artificial intelligence is charging ahead. Not even its creators know how to keep up

item.title: Elon Musk’s vision of the future
item.description: The world’s richest man sits down with The Economist

item.title: The world’s balance-sheet is out of kilter with its economy
item.description: Restoring balance could be painful

item.title: Nicaragua’s President Daniel Ortega scraps elections
item.description: The central American country may become a one-party state

item.title: Prediction markets need better rules
item.description: Their capricious settlement mechanisms are putting off serious investors

item.title: Rethinking Global Imbalances
item.description: Today’s global imbalances, starting with China's massive trade surplus, are not simply the result of poor macroeconomic coordination. Rather, they reflect long-term strategic thinking and a departure from the bias toward openness on which the postwar trade order was built.

item.title: A brief history of Luddism
item.description: States ultimately decide how fast technology is adopted

item.title: Can China dominate AI exports, too?
item.description: Robotaxis offer an early test case

item.title: The rise of the deserving rich
item.description: Today’s billionaires are more likely than yesterday’s to have made their own money

item.title: Can prediction markets win over Wall Street?
item.description: Clearing the regulatory hurdles is only half the battle

item.title: Donald Trump’s Saudi deal risks nuclear proliferation
item.description: It could have consequences far beyond the Middle East

item.title: New Delhi’s Jantar Mantar Is Not Tahrir Square, But It Can Become One
item.description: At its heart, this movement is demanding accountability, not upheaval.

item.title: The Small Suffer What They Must?
item.description: Band together or struggle alone in a disordered world.

item.title: China’s Moment of Weakness
item.description: Economic decay and America’s strategic opening.

item.title: ASEAN Foreign Ministers Call for ‘Immediate Cessation’ of Middle East Hostilities
item.description: The fighting between Iran and the United States is set to have “immediate and long-term effects” on Southeast Asia and its people, the ministers said.

item.title: Malaysia Must Reject Hate and Confront Myanmar Over the Rohingya Crisis
item.description: Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s government has largely remained silent about the recent online "hate campaign" targeting Rohingya refugees.

item.title: Philippine, Chinese Foreign Ministers Exchange Protests Over South China Sea Clash
item.description: Both nations have accused the other for instigating the incident at Second Thomas Shoal, which has overshadowed this week's ASEAN meetings in Manila.

item.title: The First Japan-Brazil Foreign Ministers’ Strategic Dialogue
item.description: An important first step, with much work still ahead.

item.title: China Coast Guard Exercises ‘Jurisdiction’ in Japan’s Exclusive Economic Zone
item.description: Japan now needs to formulate its response.

item.title: India and Japan’s Emerging Shipbuilding Partnership
item.description: The future of India-Japan security cooperation lies in shipyards – and maybe with the Mogami-class frigate.

item.title: Whatever Happened to ‘Wolf Warrior’ Diplomacy?
item.description: In recent years, Beijing has become a lot more selective in how, where, and when it deploys the assertive diplomatic tactic.
